input_row.title: Ensure Diagnostic Setting captures appropriate categories
input_row.description: Prerequisite : A Diagnostic Setting must exist. If a Diagnostic Setting does not exist, the navigation and options within this recommendation will not be available. Please review the recommendation at the beginning of this subsection titled: "Ensure that a 'Diagnostic Setting' exists." The diagnostic setting should be configured to log the appropriate activities from the control/management plane.
input_row.rationale: A diagnostic setting controls how the diagnostic log is exported. Capturing the diagnostic setting categories for appropriate control/management plane activities allows proper alerting.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Monitor. 2. Click Activity log. 3. Click on Export Activity Logs. 4. Select the appropriate Subscription. 5. Click Edit setting next to a diagnostic setting. 6. Ensure that the following categories are checked: Administrative, Alert, Policy, and Security. Audit from Azure CLI Ensure the categories 'Administrative', 'Alert', 'Policy', and 'Security' set to: 'enabled: true' az monitor diagnostic-settings subscription list --subscription <subscription ID> Audit from PowerShell Ensure the categories Administrative, Alert, Policy, and Security are set to Enabled:True Get-AzSubscriptionDiagnosticSetting -Subscription <subscriptionID> Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 3b980d31-7904-4bb7-8575-5665739a8052 - Name: 'An activity log alert should exist for specific Security operations' • Policy ID: b954148f-4c11-4c38-8221-be76711e194a - Name: 'An activity log alert should exist for specific Administrative operations' • Policy ID: c5447c04-a4d7-4ba8-a263-c9ee321a6858 - Name: 'An activity log alert should exist for specific Policy operations'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Monitor. 2. Click Activity log. 3. Click on Export Activity Logs. 4. Select the Subscription from the drop down menu. 5. Click Edit setting next to a diagnostic setting. 6. Check the following categories: Administrative, Alert, Policy, and Security. 7. Choose the destination details according to your organization's needs. 8. Click Save. Remediate from Azure CLI az monitor diagnostic-settings subscription create --subscription <subscription id> --name <diagnostic settings name> --location <location> <[- -event-hub <event hub ID> --event-hub-auth-rule <event hub auth rule ID>] [-- storage-account <storage account ID>] [--workspace <log analytics workspace ID>] --logs "[{category:Security,enabled:true},{category:Administrative,enabled:true},{ca tegory:Alert,enabled:true},{category:Policy,enabled:true}]"  Remediate from PowerShell $logCategories = @(); $logCategories += New-AzDiagnosticSettingSubscriptionLogSettingsObject - Category Administrative -Enabled $true $logCategories += New-AzDiagnosticSettingSubscriptionLogSettingsObject - Category Security -Enabled $true $logCategories += New-AzDiagnosticSettingSubscriptionLogSettingsObject - Category Alert -Enabled $true $logCategories += New-AzDiagnosticSettingSubscriptionLogSettingsObject - Category Policy -Enabled $true New-AzSubscriptionDiagnosticSetting -SubscriptionId <subscription ID> -Name <Diagnostic settings name> <[-EventHubAuthorizationRule <event hub auth rule ID> -EventHubName <event hub name>] [-StorageAccountId <storage account ID>] [-WorkSpaceId <log analytics workspace ID>] [-MarketplacePartner ID <full ARM Marketplace resource ID>]> -Log $logCategories Default Value: When the diagnostic setting is created using Azure Portal, by default no categories are selected. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/platform/diagnostic- settings 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/samples/resource- manager-diagnostic-settings 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-logging-threat- detection#lt-3-enable-logging-for-security-investigation 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/monitor/diagnostic- settings?view=azure-cli-latest 5. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/az.monitor/new- azsubscriptiondiagnosticsetting?view=azps-9.2.0

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The audit can be fully executed via Azure CLI and PowerShell commands that are clearly defined in the audit section. The control can be verified through the Azure Resource Manager (ARM) APIs that provide access to diagnostic settings.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_monitor_diagnostic_settings_audit_categories_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the following:

- **APIs and Commands to Use:**
  - Azure CLI: `az monitor diagnostic-settings subscription list --subscription <subscription ID>`
  - PowerShell: `Get-AzSubscriptionDiagnosticSetting -Subscription <subscriptionID>`

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
  - Audit will be performed across all subscriptions configured in the Azure environment.

- **Exact Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
  - The presence and configuration of the following diagnostic log categories in the diagnostic settings:
    - Administrative
    - Alert
    - Policy
    - Security

- **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** All specified categories (Administrative, Alert, Policy, and Security) are set to `enabled: true`.
  - **Fail:** One or more categories are not enabled.

- **Example Usage:**
  - For Azure CLI:
    ```bash
    az monitor diagnostic-settings subscription list --subscription <subscription ID>
    ```
    The output should show that categories Administrative, Alert, Policy, and Security are enabled.

  - For PowerShell:
    ```powershell
    Get-AzSubscriptionDiagnosticSetting -Subscription <subscriptionID>
    ```
    The output must indicate that the log settings for the listed categories are set to `Enabled: True`.

second_opinion.response:
### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as "Automated" is largely correct since the control can be verified using Azure CLI and PowerShell, which are accessible through automation scripts. However, the assessment should also consider scenarios where manual verification might be necessary, especially if there are custom diagnostic settings or if the automation fails due to permissions or API changes.
- **Azure APIs/CLI Verification:** The use of Azure CLI and PowerShell is appropriate for this task, and these tools can effectively interact with the Azure Resource Manager (ARM) APIs to retrieve diagnostic settings.

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: 8

- **Automation Details:** The commands provided are technically correct for retrieving diagnostic settings. However, the PowerShell cmdlet `Get-AzSubscriptionDiagnosticSetting` does not exist as described. The correct cmdlet should be `Get-AzDiagnosticSetting` for resource-specific settings. For subscription-level settings, a custom script might be needed to iterate over resources within the subscription.
- **Program Name Convention:** The program name `azure_monitor_diagnostic_settings_audit_categories_enabled` follows a logical naming convention but could be more concise, such as `azure_monitor_diagnostic_settings_audit`.

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS:

- **Correct PowerShell Command:** Update the PowerShell command to correctly reflect available cmdlets, such as using `Get-AzDiagnosticSetting` for specific resources.
- **Consideration of Permissions:** Ensure that the automation script checks for adequate permissions to access diagnostic settings across all subscriptions.
- **Output Parsing:** Provide examples of how to parse the output to verify that the specified categories are enabled, as this is crucial for determining pass/fail status.
- **Error Handling:** Include error handling in the automation script to manage scenarios where diagnostic settings are not found or API calls fail.

### 4. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM

- While the approach is generally sound, the incorrect PowerShell cmdlet and lack of detailed error handling reduce confidence. Correcting these issues would increase the reliability of the automated audit.

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: Modify

- **Modify:** Update the PowerShell command to use the correct cmdlet for retrieving diagnostic settings. Enhance the automation script with error handling and permission checks. Consider adding a manual verification step for edge cases where automation might not suffice.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** The compliance control can be effectively audited using Azure CLI and PowerShell, which interact with Azure Resource Manager APIs to retrieve and verify diagnostic settings. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, with the second opinion providing a correction on the PowerShell cmdlet, which enhances the technical accuracy of the approach.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_monitor_diagnostic_settings_audit_categories_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Azure Resource/Service: Azure Monitor
- API/CLI Method: `az monitor diagnostic-settings subscription list --subscription <subscription ID>`
- Properties to Check: Diagnostic log categories - Administrative, Alert, Policy, and Security
- Pass Criteria: All specified categories are set to 'enabled: true'
- Fail Criteria: Any of the specified categories are not enabled

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** The second AI opinion highlighted a technical inaccuracy in the PowerShell cmdlet name, which was corrected to `Get-AzDiagnosticSetting`. This correction aligns with the capabilities of Azure APIs, confirming the automated approach.
